1	# AGENTS.md ŌĆö Designflow BFF: Developer Guide
2	
3	Read this before touching any code. It is the authoritative guide for this repository.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## ­¤Ü© If login is broken on sandbox ŌĆö read this first
8	
9	**Symptoms reported by the user:**
10	- "Microsoft sign-in failed. Please try again."
11	- "Your session has expired. Please log in again." (including in incognito / after clearing cookies)
12	- 401 from any backend through the BFF proxy
13	- "The access token could not be verified."
14	
15	**Almost always the same root cause:** one or more of the BFF's `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars (`CORE_BACKEND_URL`, `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL`, `TRACKING_BACKEND_URL`, `DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL`) is set to a custom domain (`https://api.*.designflow.app`) instead of the Cloud Run `.run.app` URL. This breaks OIDC service-to-service auth. See ┬¦"`*_BACKEND_URL` must be `.run.app`, not the custom domain" below for the full explanation.
16	
17	**Has happened 3+ times.** The values drift because the Cloud Build trigger substitutions live in GCP (outside this repo) and someone keeps editing them back to the custom domains, thinking the `.run.app` URLs are wrong. They are not. See `~/.claude/projects/D--repos-dflow/memory/feedback_bff_oidc.md` for the full triage + fix playbook with gcloud commands.
18	
19	**Frontend changes cannot cause this symptom.** Do not waste cycles bisecting frontend commits.

22	
23	## 1. What this is
24	
25	The BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) is a lightweight Express proxy that sits between the Angular SPA and the backend microservices. Its only job is:
26	
27	1. Accept requests from the browser at `/api/:service/*`
28	2. Attach a Google Cloud Run OIDC identity token so the backend accepts the request
29	3. Proxy the request to the correct backend service
30	4. Forward the response back
31	
32	The BFF is the only service that is `--allow-unauthenticated` from the browser's perspective. All backend services require the BFF's OIDC token.
33	
34	> **Note on "OIDC" in this repo:** OIDC here refers exclusively to Google Cloud service-to-service authentication ŌĆö the BFF proving its identity to Cloud Run backend services. It has nothing to do with user login. User authentication is handled separately via Microsoft Azure AD (MSAL). These are two unrelated systems that happen to share the word "token."
35	
36	---
37	
38	## 2. Repository structure
39	
40	```
41	src/server.js           App bootstrap, CORS, env validation, startup logging
42	src/serviceMap.js       Maps { core, item_master, tracking, data_sync } ŌåÆ env URLs
43	src/backendProxy.js     OIDC token generation + header swap (the core security logic)
44	routes/api.js           Generic proxy handler for /api/:service/*
45	routes/health.js        GET /health
46	routes/index.js         Router mounting
47	config/env.js           Loads .env.${NODE_ENV} via dotenv
48	env/
49	  .env.sandbox-albert   Albert's personal sandbox environment
50	  .env.sandbox          Shared staging/sandbox environment
51	  .env.production       Production environment
52	  .env.example          Template ŌĆö copy to create a new env file
53	tests/unit/             Jest unit tests (matched by jest.config.js)
54	test/                   Legacy smoke tests ŌĆö not part of Jest (separate runner)
55	```

132	## 9. Intentional quirks
133	
134	### `*_BACKEND_URL` must be `.run.app`, not the custom domain
135	
136	**Looks like:** Using `https://popcre-core-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` instead of `https://api.core.designflow.app` ŌĆö looks like a misconfiguration.
137	
138	**Actually:** `src/backendProxy.js` uses the backend URL as both the proxy target AND the OIDC token audience. Cloud Run verifies the token audience against the service's `.run.app` URL. Custom domain mappings do not work as OIDC audiences in practice, even though they appear in Cloud Run domain mapping configuration.
139	
140	**Why:** Discovered 2026-05-26 when SSO broke across all environments after BFF redeployments. Switching to `.run.app` URLs resolved it immediately.
141	
142	**Do not change because:** Switching back to custom domain URLs will cause `401 The access token could not be verified` on all backend requests through the BFF.
143	
144	---
145	
146	### `x-user-authorization` header convention
147	
148	**Looks like:** A non-standard header. Why not just use `Authorization`?
149	
150	**Actually:** The BFF puts the Google OIDC token in `Authorization` (Cloud Run reads this for auth). The user's Designflow JWT goes to `X-User-Authorization`. The backend's first middleware in `index.js` swaps it back: if `x-user-authorization` is present, it overwrites `authorization` before any route handler runs.
151	
152	**Why:** Cloud Run's auth layer reads `Authorization`. If the user JWT were there, Cloud Run would reject it (wrong signature). The OIDC token must be in `Authorization` for the Cloud Run layer; the user JWT travels in a separate header and is restored by the backend.
153	
154	**Do not change because:** Changing the header name requires a coordinated update to the backend's `index.js` swap middleware.
155	
156	---
157	
158	### `isLocalHttpBackend` skips OIDC for local URLs
159	
160	**Looks like:** Inconsistency ŌĆö some requests get OIDC tokens, others don't.
161	
162	**Actually:** `src/backendProxy.js` checks `u.protocol === 'http:' && (hostname === 'localhost' || hostname === '127.0.0.1')`. If true, it skips OIDC token generation entirely and passes the original Authorization header unchanged.
163	
164	**Why:** The GCP metadata server isn't available in local development. Skipping OIDC for local HTTP backends lets developers run against a local backend without needing GCP credentials.
165	
166	**Do not change because:** Removing this check would break local development.
167	
168	---

204	## 12. Incidents
205	
206	### 2026-05-26 ŌĆö Microsoft SSO failed due to Google Cloud OIDC audience mismatch
207	
208	**Symptom:** Microsoft sign-in appeared to succeed (popup opened and closed) but the app showed "Microsoft sign-in failed." `POST /api/core/auth/azure` returned `401`. Cloud Run logged: `The request was not authorized to invoke this service. The access token could not be verified.`
209	
210	**What broke and why:** Microsoft SSO itself was fine ŌĆö Azure AD authenticated the user correctly. The failure was in the next step: the BFF proxying the user's Azure token to the backend. The BFF attaches a Google Cloud OIDC token to every backend request so Cloud Run accepts it (see ┬¦1). That Google OIDC token had the wrong `audience` claim because `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to the custom domain (`https://api.core.*.designflow.app`). Cloud Run validates OIDC token audiences against the service's `.run.app` URL only ŌĆö custom domains are not accepted, even when they correctly route traffic to the service.
211	
212	**Why it appeared suddenly:** The BFF was redeployed (revision 00007, 2026-05-25) adding the OIDC token attachment code (`src/backendProxy.js`). Before that revision, the BFF sent no Authorization header to the backend, causing a different error (`Empty Authorization header`). The OIDC audience was never validated before because OIDC was never used before.
213	
214	**Fix:** Updated all four `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars in all three BFF Cloud Run services to `.run.app` service URLs via `gcloud run services update --update-env-vars`. Also updated `env/.env.*` files in this repo to match.
215	
216	**Commands run:**
217	```bash
218	gcloud run services update popcre-albert-bff-sandbox --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
219	  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-item-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-tracking-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-sync-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"

1	# Designflow BFF
2	
3	Backend-for-Frontend service for Designflow. Provides a single authenticated proxy entry point for the Angular SPA and forwards requests to the correct backend service (`core`, `item_master`, `tracking`, `data_sync`).
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Stack
8	
9	- Node.js + Express
10	- `http-proxy` for request forwarding
11	- `google-auth-library` for Cloud Run OIDC token generation
12	
13	---
14	
15	## How it works
16	
17	```
18	Browser ŌåÆ BFF (api.*.designflow.app)
19	  ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ BFF attaches Google OIDC token ŌåÆ backend Cloud Run service
20	        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Backend verifies token, serves request
21	```
22	
23	The BFF runs on Cloud Run as a service account (`designflow-bff-invoker@...` for sandbox environments, `deployer@...` for production). Both have `roles/run.invoker` at the GCP project level, so they can call any backend service.
24	
25	For every proxied request, `src/backendProxy.js`:
26	1. Fetches an OIDC identity token from the GCP metadata server for the target backend service URL
27	2. Sets `Authorization: Bearer <oidc-token>` (for Cloud Run authentication)
28	3. Moves the original user JWT to `X-User-Authorization` (the backend remaps this back to `Authorization` in its first middleware)
29	
30	---
31	
32	## Critical: `*_BACKEND_URL` must be the `.run.app` service URL
33	
34	**Do not set any `*_BACKEND_URL` to a custom domain (e.g. `https://api.core.*.designflow.app`).**
35	
36	Cloud Run verifies an incoming OIDC token by checking its `aud` claim against the service's URL. In practice, this means the `*.run.app` URL ŌĆö **not** a custom domain mapped to the service. Even though Cloud Run domain mappings are configured and the custom domain resolves correctly, using a custom domain as the OIDC token audience results in:
37	
38	```
39	The request was not authorized to invoke this service. The access token could not be verified.
40	```
41	
42	This caused the 2026-05-26 SSO outage (see `AGENTS.md` ┬¦Incidents). All three environments were updated to use `.run.app` URLs.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## Project layout
47	
48	```
49	src/server.js           App bootstrap, CORS, env validation
50	src/serviceMap.js       Maps service keys ŌåÆ backend URLs from env
51	src/backendProxy.js     OIDC token attachment; header swap logic
52	routes/api.js           Generic route /api/:service/*
53	routes/health.js        GET /health
54	routes/index.js         Mounts api and health routers
55	config/env.js           Loads .env.${NODE_ENV}
56	env/                    Per-environment .env files
57	  .env.sandbox-albert   Albert's sandbox
58	  .env.sandbox          Shared sandbox
59	  .env.production       Production
60	```
61	
62	## Documentation
63	
64	| Doc | Contents |
65	|---|---|
66	| [`docs/system-api-inventory.md`](docs/system-api-inventory.md) | System-level API inventory across Designflow services |
67	| [`docs/api-reference.md`](docs/api-reference.md) | BFF gateway routes, service keys, auth/proxy behavior |
68	
69	---
70	
71	## Cloud Run services and URLs
72	
73	| Environment | BFF service | BFF domain | Core backend service | Core backend URL (for OIDC) |
74	|---|---|---|---|---|
75	| Albert's sandbox | `popcre-albert-bff-sandbox` | `api.alsand.designflow.app` | `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox` | `https://popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` |
76	| Shared sandbox | `popcre-bff-sandbox` | `api.sandbox.designflow.app` | `popcre-core-sandbox` | `https://popcre-core-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` |
77	| Production | `popcre-bff-prod` | `api.designflow.app` | `popcre-core-prod` | `https://popcre-core-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` |
78	
79	GCP project: `lithe-breaker-323913`, region: `us-central1`.
80	
81	If a backend service is redeployed and gets a new `.run.app` URL, update the Cloud Run env var via:
82	```bash
83	gcloud run services update <bff-service> \
84	  --region=us-central1 \
85	  --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
86	  --update-env-vars CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://<new-url>.run.app
87	```
88	And update the corresponding `env/.env.*` file.
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Local development
93	
94	```bash
95	npm install
96	cp env/.env.example env/.env.local   # fill in real values; use localhost backend URLs
97	NODE_ENV=local node src/server.js
98	```
99	
100	Local `.env` files with `http://localhost:*` backend URLs automatically skip OIDC token generation (`isLocalHttpBackend` check in `src/backendProxy.js`).
101	
102	---
103	
104	## Run (Cloud environments)
105	
106	```bash
107	npm run start:sandbox-albert   # Albert's sandbox
108	npm run start:sandbox          # Shared sandbox
109	npm run start:production       # Production
110	```
111	
112	Each script runs `npm test` first, then starts `nodemon`. Tests must pass or the process exits.
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Tests
117	
118	```bash
119	npm test                          # Jest unit tests (tests/**/*.test.js)
120	npx jest --watch                  # Watch mode
121	npx jest tests/unit/sanity.test.js  # Single file
122	```
123	
124	---
125	
126	## API contract
127	
128	- `GET /health` ŌåÆ `{ status: 'ok' }`
129	- `ALL /api/:service/*` ŌåÆ proxied to the backend identified by `:service`
130	
131	Valid service keys: `core`, `item_master`, `tracking`, `data_sync`. Unknown key ŌåÆ 404.
132	
133	Route example: `POST /api/core/auth/azure` ŌåÆ proxied to `<CORE_BACKEND_URL>/auth/azure`.
134	
135	---
136	
137	## System interconnectivity
138	
139	This repo is the public API gateway for the Designflow PLM system. It is the
140	connection point between the Angular SPA and the private core, item-master,
141	tracking, and data-sync services. See
142	[`docs/system-interconnectivity.md`](docs/system-interconnectivity.md) for the
143	BFF-specific map and [`docs/system-architecture.md`](docs/system-architecture.md)
144	for the full multi-repo system map.

1	# Test Setup Guide
2	
3	## Google Cloud Authentication Setup
4	
5	To test token generation, you need to configure Google Cloud credentials. Choose one of the following methods:
6	
7	### Option 1: Service Account Key File (Recommended for Local Development)
8	
9	1. Create or download a service account key from Google Cloud Console:
10	   - Go to [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/)
11	   - Navigate to IAM & Admin > Service Accounts
12	   - Create or select a service account
13	   - Create a new key (JSON format)
14	   - Download the key file
15	
16	2. Set the environment variable:
17	   ```bash
18	   # Windows PowerShell
19	   $env:GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="C:\path\to\your\service-account-key.json"
20	   
21	   # Windows CMD
22	   set GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=C:\path\to\your\service-account-key.json
23	   
24	   # Linux/Mac
25	   export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/your/service-account-key.json"
26	   ```
27	
28	3. Add to your `.env.localhost` file (optional, but recommended):
29	   ```
30	   GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=C:\path\to\your\service-account-key.json
31	   ```
32	
33	### Option 2: gcloud Application Default Credentials
34	
35	1. Install Google Cloud SDK if you haven't already:
36	   - Download from: https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install
37	
38	2. Authenticate using your user account:
39	   ```bash
40	   gcloud auth application-default login
41	   ```
42	
43	3. This will open a browser for authentication and store credentials locally.
44	
45	**Troubleshooting gcloud auth:**
46	- If you get "scope is required but not consented" error:
47	  - Make sure to check ALL permission boxes when the browser opens
48	  - Try clearing existing credentials first: `gcloud auth application-default revoke`
49	  - Then run the login command again
50	  - If browser doesn't open, try: `gcloud auth login` first, then `gcloud auth application-default login`
51	
52	### Option 3: Running in GCP Environment
53	
54	If you're running this in a GCP environment (Cloud Run, GCE, GKE, etc.), credentials are automatically available.
55	
56	## Running Tests
57	
58	```bash
59	# Test token generation
60	npm run test:token
61	
62	# Test full proxy server
63	npm run test:proxy
64	```
65	
66	## Environment Variables
67	
68	Make sure your `.env.localhost` file includes:
69	
70	```env
71	CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://your-core-service.run.app
72	ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://your-item-master-service.run.app
73	TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://your-tracking-service.run.app
74	DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://your-data-sync-service.run.app
75	GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=C:\path\to\service-account-key.json
76	```
77	
78	## Troubleshooting
79	
80	### Error: "Could not load the default credentials"
81	
82	- Verify `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` points to a valid JSON file
83	- Or run `gcloud auth application-default login`
84	- Check that the service account has necessary permissions
85	- **Recommended**: Use Option 1 (Service Account Key) for more reliable local development
86	
87	### Error: "scope is required but not consented" (gcloud auth)
88	
89	- When the browser opens, make sure to check ALL permission boxes
90	- Try revoking existing credentials first:
91	  ```bash
92	  gcloud auth application-default revoke
93	  gcloud auth application-default login
94	  ```
95	- If issues persist, use a service account key file (Option 1) instead
96	
97	### Error: "Permission denied" or "Access denied"
98	
99	- Ensure the service account has the `Service Account Token Creator` role
100	- For Cloud Run services, the service account needs permission to invoke the target service
101	- Verify the service account email matches the one used in your GCP project

1	# Designflow BFF API Reference
2	
3	The BFF is the public HTTP entry point for the Angular SPA. It proxies requests
4	to private backend Cloud Run services and handles service-to-service identity.
5	
6	## Base URLs
7	
8	| Environment | Base URL |
9	|---|---|
10	| Local | `http://localhost:5004` |
11	| Albert sandbox | `https://api.alsand.designflow.app` |
12	| Shared sandbox | `https://api.sandbox.designflow.app` |
13	| Production | `https://api.designflow.app` |
14	
15	## Auth
16	
17	Browser callers send the user JWT in `Authorization`.
18	
19	For proxied requests, `src/backendProxy.js`:
20	
21	1. fetches a Google OIDC token for the target Cloud Run service
22	2. sets `Authorization` to the OIDC token
23	3. moves the original user JWT to `X-User-Authorization`
24	
25	Backend services then copy `X-User-Authorization` back to `Authorization` before
26	running their normal JWT role checks.
27	
28	## Routes
29	
30	| Method | Path | Description |
31	|---|---|---|
32	| `GET` | `/health` | BFF health check |
33	| `ALL` | `/api/:service/*` | Proxy to the backend service identified by `:service` |
34	
35	## Service Keys
36	
37	| Service key | Environment variable | Target repo |
38	|---|---|---|
39	| `core` | `CORE_BACKEND_URL` | `designflow-backend` |
40	| `item_master` | `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL` | `designflow-item-master` |
41	| `tracking` | `TRACKING_BACKEND_URL` | `designflow-tracking` |
42	| `data_sync` | `DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL` | `designflow-data-syncing` |
43	
44	## Examples
45	
46	```text
47	POST /api/core/auth/azure
48	GET  /api/core/api/customers
49	POST /api/core/getAllItem
50	GET  /api/item_master/lib/getAllitems
51	POST /api/item_master/detail/getItemInfo
52	GET  /api/tracking/sample/list
53	GET  /api/data_sync/getItemHeaderFromCL
54	```
55	
56	Unknown service keys return `404`. Requests with no backend path also return
57	`404`.
58	
59	## Contract Status
60	
61	This is a gateway contract. The BFF path shape is stable, but request and response
62	bodies are owned by the downstream services.
63	
64

1	# Designflow API Inventory
2	
3	Designflow contains a multi-service API system. The Angular frontend does not
4	call the backend services directly in deployed environments. It calls the BFF,
5	and the BFF proxies each request to the correct backend service.
6	
7	## Production Entry Point
8	
9	| Caller | Base URL |
10	|---|---|
11	| Browser / frontend | `https://api.designflow.app` |
12	| Local frontend | `http://localhost:5004` |
13	
14	The BFF exposes:
15	
16	```text
17	/api/core/*        -> designflow-backend
18	/api/item_master/* -> designflow-item-master
19	/api/tracking/*    -> designflow-tracking
20	/api/data_sync/*   -> designflow-data-syncing
21	/health            -> BFF health
22	```
23	
24	## Services
25	
26	| Repo | API role | Auth model | Local default port |
27	|---|---|---|---|
28	| `designflow-bff` | Public API gateway for the SPA | User JWT from browser plus Cloud Run OIDC to services | `5004` |
29	| `designflow-backend` | Core PLM/RFQ/admin/auth/file API | User JWT via `Authorization` or `X-User-Authorization` | `5000` |
30	| `designflow-item-master` | Item library, item detail, art piece API | User JWT via `Authorization` or `X-User-Authorization` | `5003` |
31	| `designflow-tracking` | Production, licensing, lead-time, sample tracking API | User JWT via `Authorization` or `X-User-Authorization` | `5002` |
32	| `designflow-data-syncing` | Coldlion/system sync API | `X-API-Key` | `5001` |
33	| `designflow-frontend` | API consumer, not an API provider | Sends JWT to the BFF | `4200` |
34	| `infrastructure` | Deployment/IaC repo | Not checked out locally | n/a |
35	
36	## Important Boundary
37	
38	Designflow already has APIs, but they are mostly internal application APIs shaped
39	around the Angular frontend. They are not yet a polished external/partner API:
40	
41	- no single OpenAPI spec
42	- inconsistent route naming across legacy endpoints
43	- many request/response bodies are raw domain rows or grid-specific payloads
44	- BFF routes are stable as a gateway, but backend route contracts are not all
45	  documented as public contracts
46	
47	For external applications, add a stable `/api/external/*` surface with DTOs.
48	For AI sessions, add an MCP server that calls the existing APIs/services through
49	read-only, role-aware tools.
50	
51	## Service API Docs
52	
53	| Repo | API doc |
54	|---|---|
55	| `designflow-bff` | `designflow-bff/docs/api-reference.md` |
56	| `designflow-backend` | `designflow-backend/docs/api-reference.md` |
57	| `designflow-item-master` | `designflow-item-master/docs/api-reference.md` |
58	| `designflow-tracking` | `designflow-tracking/docs/api-reference.md` |
59	| `designflow-data-syncing` | `designflow-data-syncing/docs/api-reference.md` |
60	| `designflow-frontend` | `designflow-frontend/docs/api-consumption.md` |
61	
62	## Cross-repo system map
63	
64	For the full Designflow PLM architecture and feature dependency matrix, see the
65	workspace-level docs:
66	
67	- [`system-architecture.md`](system-architecture.md)
68	- [`feature-dependency-matrix.md`](feature-dependency-matrix.md)
69	
70	The BFF-specific interconnectivity notes live in
71	[`system-interconnectivity.md`](system-interconnectivity.md).

/api/:service/*"] 13 Core["designflow-backend"] 14 ItemMaster["designflow-item-master"] 15 Tracking["designflow-tracking"] 16 Sync["designflow-data-syncing"] 17 18 FE --> BFF 19 BFF -->|/api/core/*| Core 20 BFF -->|/api/item_master/*| ItemMaster 21 BFF -->|/api/tracking/*| Tracking 22 BFF -->|/api/data_sync/*| Sync 23 ``` 24 25 ## Service Keys 26 27 | Service key | Target repo | Purpose | 28 |---|---|---| 29 | `core` | `designflow-backend` | RFQ, auth, admin, notifications, standardized products, tariffs, file helpers | 30 | `item_master` | `designflow-item-master` | Item Library, Item Detail, Art Piece | 31 | `tracking` | `designflow-tracking` | Production, licensing, lead-time, sample tracking | 32 | `data_sync` | `designflow-data-syncing` | Coldlion/system synchronization | 33 34 ## Header Contract 35 36 The browser sends: 37 38 ```text 39 Authorization: Bearer 40 ``` 41 42 For private Cloud Run targets, the BFF sends: 43 44 ```text 45 Authorization: Bearer 46 X-User-Authorization: Bearer 47 ``` 48 49 Every private service has early middleware that copies `X-User-Authorization` 50 back to `Authorization` so existing `authRole(...)` middleware continues to 51 validate the Designflow JWT. 52 53 ## Deployment Coupling 54 55 The `*_BACKEND_URL` environment variables must point to the private service 56 `.run.app` URL. ARG NODE_ENV
ENV NODE_ENV $NODE_ENV

WORKDIR /app

COPY . .

# Install all deps (including devDependencies) so start scripts can run (cross-env, nodemon)
RUN mkdir -p .yarn-cache && yarn install --cache-folder .yarn-cache --production=false

EXPOSE 5004

CMD yarn start:$NODE_ENV
# BFF Cloud Run deploy. Trigger must supply substitutions (see cloudbuild-triggers.sh).
# Sandbox: _APP_ENV=sandbox, _API_ADDRESS=https://api.sandbox.designflow.app, backend URLs = each private Cloud Run HTTPS URL.
steps:
  # ­¤ō” Build the image using Dockerfile
  - id: Build the image
    name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
    args:
      - build
      - -t
      - ${_IMAGE}
      - --build-arg=NODE_ENV=${_APP_ENV}
      - .

  # ­¤öā Push the built image to artifact registry
  - id: Push the image to artifact registry
    name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
    args:
      - push
      - ${_IMAGE}

  # Env for Cloud Run: YAML file so commas in CORS_ORIGINS are safe (unlike --set-env-vars). Substitutions are expanded by Cloud Build before bash runs.
  - id: Create Cloud Run env file
    name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk'
    entrypoint: bash
    args:
      - -c
      - |
        cat <<ENVEOF >/workspace/run-env.yaml
        NODE_ENV: "${_APP_ENV}"
        API_ADDRESS: "${_API_ADDRESS}"
        CORS_ORIGINS: "${_CORS_ORIGINS}"
        CORE_BACKEND_URL: "${_CORE_BACKEND_URL}"
        ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL: "${_ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL}"
        TRACKING_BACKEND_URL: "${_TRACKING_BACKEND_URL}"
        DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL: "${_DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL}"
        ENVEOF

  - id: Deploy the image to cloud run
    name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk'
    entrypoint: gcloud
    args:
      - run
      - deploy
      - ${_SERVICE_NAME}
      - --image=${_IMAGE}
      - --region=${_REGION}
      - --port=${_PORT}
      - --concurrency=${_CONCURRENCY}
      - --max-instances=${_MAX_INSTANCES}
      - --allow-unauthenticated
      - --platform=${_PLATFORM}
      - --service-account=${_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME}
      - --env-vars-file=/workspace/run-env.yaml

substitutions:
  _REGION: $$_REGION
  _SERVICE_NAME: $$_SERVICE_NAME
  _APP_ENV: $$_APP_ENV
  _APP_NAME: $$_APP_NAME
  _ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME: $$_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME
  _SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME: $$_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME
  _PORT: $$_PORT

  _ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL: $$_ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL

  _DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL: $$_DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL
  _API_ADDRESS: $$_API_ADDRESS
  _CORS_ORIGINS: $$_CORS_ORIGINS
  _IMAGE: ${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/${_APP_ENV}/${_APP_NAME}:${BUILD_ID}-${COMMIT_SHA}

1 # Designflow PLM System Architecture 2 3 This document maps the Designflow product lifecycle management system across the 4 local repositories under `D:\repos\dflow`. It is written from the code as it 5 exists in this workspace, not from the GitHub repo names alone. 6 7 The important correction to the informal "five repo" model is that the runtime 8 PLM system has six active application/service repos locally: 9 10 - `designflow-frontend` 11 - `designflow-bff` 12 - `designflow-backend` 13 - `designflow-item-master` 14 - `designflow-tracking` 15 - `designflow-data-syncing` 16 17 The original five named repos are the main subject, but `designflow-item-master` 18 is an active service in the request path. The frontend calls it through the BFF, 19 and the BFF has a first-class `item_master` service mapping. Any complete system 20 map should include it. 21 22 ## Executive Summary 23 24 Designflow is a Cloud Run based PLM application. The browser runs a static 25 Angular SPA. The SPA does not call private backend services directly in deployed 26 environments; it calls the BFF at `api.*.designflow.app`. The BFF proxies each 27 request to one of the private domain services, adding a Cloud Run OIDC token for 28 service-to-service invocation while preserving the user's Designflow JWT in 29 `X-User-Authorization`. 30 31 Most services use: 32 33 - Node.js CommonJS 34 - Express 35 - PostgreSQL 36 - Sequelize 37 - `SCHEMA` to select the logical database schema 38 - RS256 Designflow JWTs for user identity and role checks 39 40 The main persistent coupling between services is the shared PostgreSQL schema. 41 The HTTP/API coupling is intentionally centralized through the BFF. 42 43 ## High-Level Runtime View 44 45 ```mermaid 46 flowchart LR 47 Browser["Browser
Angular SPA
designflow-frontend"] 48 BFF["Public BFF / API Gateway
designflow-bff
Cloud Run public
local :5004"] 49 Core["Core PLM API
designflow-backend
Cloud Run private
local :5000"] 50 ItemMaster["Item Master API
designflow-item-master
Cloud Run private
local :5003"] 51 Tracking["Tracking API
designflow-tracking
Cloud Run private
local :5002"] 52 Sync["Data Sync API
designflow-data-syncing
Cloud Run private
local :5001"] 53 DB["PostgreSQL
shared PLM schema"] 54 Spaces["DigitalOcean Spaces
images, files, sample photos"] 55 Coldlion["Coldlion
external system of record / sync source"] 56 Azure["Microsoft Azure AD
SSO identity"] 57 OpenRouter["OpenRouter
AI HTS classification proxy"] 58 CROSS["CBP CROSS
rulings search"] 59 Brevo["Brevo
transactional email"] 60 61 Browser -->|"Authorization: Designflow JWT"| BFF 62 BFF -->|"Authorization: Cloud Run OIDC
X-User-Authorization: Designflow JWT"| Core 63 BFF -->|"Authorization: Cloud Run OIDC
X-User-Authorization: Designflow JWT"| ItemMaster 64 BFF -->|"Authorization: Cloud Run OIDC
X-User-Authorization: Designflow JWT"| Tracking 65 BFF -->|"Cloud Run OIDC + X-API-Key where required"| Sync 66 67 Core --> DB 68 ItemMaster --> DB 69 Tracking --> DB 70 Sync --> DB 71 72 Core --> Spaces 73 ItemMaster --> Spaces 74 Tracking --> Spaces 75 76 Sync <--> Coldlion 77 Core <--> Azure 78 Core --> OpenRouter 79 Core --> CROSS 80 Core --> Brevo 81 ``` 82 83 ## Public and Private Boundaries 84 85 ```mermaid 86 flowchart TB 87 subgraph Public["Public ingress"] 88 Frontend["designflow-frontend
static Angular app"] 89 BFF["designflow-bff
api.designflow.app
api.sandbox.designflow.app
api.alsand.designflow.app"] 90 end 91 92 subgraph Private["Private Cloud Run services"] 93 Core["designflow-backend"] 94 ItemMaster["designflow-item-master"] 95 Tracking["designflow-tracking"] 96 DataSync["designflow-data-syncing"] 97 end 98 99 subgraph Data["Persistent / external data"] 100 Postgres["PostgreSQL"] 101 DO["DigitalOcean Spaces"] 102 Coldlion["Coldlion"] 103 end 104 105 Frontend --> BFF 106 BFF --> Core 107 BFF --> ItemMaster 108 BFF --> Tracking 109 BFF --> DataSync 110 Core --> Postgres 111 ItemMaster --> Postgres 112 Tracking --> Postgres 113 DataSync --> Postgres 114 Core --> DO 115 ItemMaster --> DO 116 Tracking --> DO 117 DataSync <--> Coldlion 118 ``` 119 120 The BFF is intentionally public. The domain services are deployed with 121 `--no-allow-unauthenticated` and are invoked by the BFF using Cloud Run service 122 identity. The frontend Cloud Run service is public because it serves the static 123 SPA. 124 125 ## Repository Responsibilities 126 127 ### `designflow-frontend` 128 129 The frontend is the Angular 21 SPA. It owns: 130 131 - UI routing and feature modules 132 - Role-based client navigation and route guards 133 - AG Grid based work surfaces 134 - RFQ calculation behavior in the browser 135 - User JWT storage in `localStorage` 136 - HTTP interceptors for auth, loading, and error reporting 137 - Microsoft SSO browser flow before exchanging the Azure ID token for a 138 Designflow JWT 139 140 The deployed frontend calls the BFF, not individual backend services directly. 141 The environment files define these service bases: 142 143 - `environment.url` -> `/api/core` 144 - `environment.item_master` -> `/api/item_master` 145 - `environment.tracking` -> `/api/tracking` 146 - `environment.data_sync` -> `/api/data_sync` 147 148 Major user-facing modules: 149 150 - RFQ 151 - Item Library 152 - Item Detail 153 - Art Piece management 154 - Standardized Products 155 - Production Tracking 156 - Licensing Tracking 157 - Sample Tracking 158 - Lead Time Calculator 159 - Admin / Editor 160 - User Management and impersonation 161 - Notifications 162 - HTS / tariff / AI classification UI 163 164 ### `designflow-bff` 165 166 The BFF is the public API gateway for the SPA. It owns: 167 168 - `/health` 169 - `/api/:service/*` 170 - service key mapping 171 - Cloud Run OIDC identity token generation 172 - original user JWT forwarding 173 - deployed CORS configuration 174 175 Service keys: 176 177 - `core` -> `designflow-backend` 178 - `item_master` -> `designflow-item-master` 179 - `tracking` -> `designflow-tracking` 180 - `data_sync` -> `designflow-data-syncing` 181 182 The BFF strips the service prefix before proxying. For example: 183 184 ```text 185 Browser: 186 GET /api/tracking/sample/list 187 188 BFF target: 189 GET /sample/list on designflow-tracking 190 ``` 191 192 Cloud Run OIDC audience must be the backend service `.run.app` URL, not the 193 custom `api.*.designflow.app` domain. This is why the BFF environment variables 194 must use backend `.run.app` URLs. 195 196 ### `designflow-backend` 197 198 The core backend owns broad PLM business logic and shared administration. It is 199 the densest service and includes both legacy root routes and newer `/api/*` 200 routes. 201 202 It owns: 203 204 - Azure SSO token exchange and Designflow JWT issuing 205 - Password/signup/single-use token flows 206 - RFQ grid and vendor quote workflows 207 - RFQ view/grid layout persistence 208 - Factory/vendor management support 209 - Customers, item sizes, item depths, delivery locations, FOB countries 210 - Standardized product APIs 211 - Comments and audit logs 212 - Notifications and mention notifications 213 - Admin app settings 214 - Tariff management: MFN HTS lookup, Section 301, Trump 2025 rates 215 - OpenRouter proxy for AI classification 216 - CBP CROSS proxy 217 - Brevo transactional email 218 - DigitalOcean Spaces presign/public URL helpers 219 - Impersonation tokens 220 221 Representative route groups: 222 223 - `/auth` 224 - root legacy RFQ routes such as `/getAllItem`, `/updateRfqItem`, 225 `/updateVendor`, `/sendPriceReq` 226 - `/std` 227 - `/api/admin` 228 - `/api/openrouter` 229 - `/api/cross` 230 - `/api/comment` 231 - `/api/notifications` 232 - `/api/user` 233 - `/api/customers` 234 - `/api/grid` 235 - root DigitalOcean routes such as `/getPreSignedUrl`, `/setObjectPublic` 236 237 ### `designflow-item-master` 238 239 This service is not in the original five-link prompt, but it is wired into the 240 frontend and BFF as an active service. It owns item master workflows that are no 241 longer solely in the core backend. 242 243 It owns: 244 245 - Item Library APIs under `/lib` 246 - Item Detail APIs under `/detail` 247 - Art Piece APIs under `/artPiece` 248 - Item creation and item type lookup 249 - Product assignment APIs 250 - Item and art piece media/attachment workflows 251 - Item detail licensing status helpers 252 253 Representative frontend consumers: 254 255 - `ItemLibraryService` 256 - `ItemDetailService` 257 - `ArtPieceService` 258 259 ### `designflow-tracking` 260 261 Tracking is a separate private service for lifecycle tracking after or around 262 product/item creation. 263 264 It owns: 265 266 - Production tracking root routes 267 - Licensing tracking routes under `/lic` 268 - Lead-time lookup and calculation under `/lead-time` 269 - Sample tracking under `/sample` 270 - Sample events, boxes, groups, comments, photos 271 - DigitalOcean Spaces upload flow for sample photos 272 - Startup caches for licensing and factory lead-time calculations 273 274 Representative route groups: 275 276 - `/getProdOrderHeader` 277 - `/updateProdOrderHeader` 278 - `/factoryProdOrders` 279 - `/factoryProdOrderDates` 280 - `/syncPrepackAssociations` 281 - `/lic/getLic` 282 - `/lic/groups` 283 - `/lead-time/calculate` 284 - `/sample/list` 285 - `/sample/events` 286 - `/sample/boxes` 287 - `/sample/photos` 288 289 ### `designflow-data-syncing` 290 291 Data Sync is a private system-to-system integration service for Coldlion sync 292 flows. It is not a normal user-facing route surface. 293 294 It owns: 295 296 - Pulling item headers from Coldlion 297 - Pulling item details from Coldlion 298 - Pulling merch group headers/details 299 - Pulling production order headers/details 300 - Pulling external vendors/customers 301 - Syncing prepack associations 302 303 It uses `X-API-Key: ` for every request, including health. 304 It also has Coldlion API credentials injected through secrets. 305 306 ## Request and Auth Flow 307 308 ```mermaid 309 sequenceDiagram 310 participant U as User / Browser 311 participant FE as Angular SPA 312 participant BFF as designflow-bff 313 participant SVC as Private backend service 314 participant DB as PostgreSQL 315 316 U->>FE: Uses PLM feature 317 FE->>BFF: HTTP request with Authorization: Bearer DesignflowJWT 318 BFF->>BFF: Resolve service key from /api/:service/* 319 BFF->>BFF: Fetch Google OIDC token for backend .run.app URL 320 BFF->>SVC: Authorization: Bearer CloudRunOIDC 321 BFF->>SVC: X-User-Authorization: Bearer DesignflowJWT 322 SVC->>SVC: Copy X-User-Authorization back to Authorization 323 SVC->>SVC: authRole validates RS256 Designflow JWT and role 324 SVC->>DB: Sequelize query/update 325 DB-->>SVC: Rows 326 SVC-->>BFF: JSON response 327 BFF-->>FE: JSON response 328 ``` 329 330 This dual-token pattern is the key to understanding deployed authentication: 331 332 - Cloud Run sees the OIDC token and allows the BFF to invoke the private service. 333 - Express route guards see the restored user JWT and enforce product roles. 334 335 ## Deployment Model 336 337 Cloud Build builds each repo into a container image and deploys it to Cloud Run. 338 339 Public services: 340 341 - `designflow-frontend`: public static SPA 342 - `designflow-bff`: public API entrypoint 343 344 Private services: 345 346 - `designflow-backend` 347 - `designflow-item-master` 348 - `designflow-tracking` 349 - `designflow-data-syncing` 350 351 Common private service deployment traits: 352 353 - VPC connector configured 354 - `--no-allow-unauthenticated` 355 - DB secrets injected from Secret Manager 356 - `SCHEMA` injected through environment substitutions 357 358 The core backend additionally injects: 359 360 - JWT private/public keys 361 - DigitalOcean Spaces keys 362 - Brevo API key 363 - OpenRouter / DeepSeek keys 364 - Azure client/tenant IDs 365 366 The data-sync service additionally injects: 367 368 - `DFLOW_SYNC_API_KEY` 369 - `COLDLION_API_KEY` 370 371 ## Shared Data Model and Coupling 372 373 The services are split by API ownership, but they are strongly connected through 374 the same database schema. Shared model families include: 375 376 - Users, roles, permissions 377 - Vendors/factories 378 - Customers 379 - Merch groups 380 - UDF tables/elements/components 381 - Grid layouts and grid access 382 - Item header/detail/attachments 383 - RFQ items, groups, vendors, steps, statuses, containers 384 - Standardized products 385 - Production order header/detail 386 - Licensing status/milestone/feed-back/time records 387 - Sample tracking tables 388 - External customers/vendors 389 390 This means a feature often crosses service boundaries through common table 391 identifiers even when there is no direct service-to-service HTTP call. For 392 example, Sample Tracking stores `item_id_fk` and `prod_order_no_fk` links that 393 connect samples back to item master and production records. 394 395 ## Feature Area Map 396 397 ```mermaid 398 flowchart TB 399 subgraph UX["Frontend feature modules"] 400 RFQ["RFQ"] 401 IL["Item Library"] 402 ID["Item Detail"] 403 AP["Art Piece"] 404 STD["Standardized Products"] 405 PT["Production Tracking"] 406 LT["Licensing Tracking"] 407 ST["Sample Tracking"] 408 LTC["Lead Time"] 409 Admin["Admin / Editor"] 410 end 411 412 RFQ --> Core["Core backend"] 413 STD --> Core 414 Admin --> Core 415 IL --> ItemMaster["Item Master"] 416 ID --> ItemMaster 417 AP --> ItemMaster 418 PT --> Tracking["Tracking"] 419 LT --> Tracking 420 ST --> Tracking 421 LTC --> Tracking 422 423 Core --> DB["PostgreSQL"] 424 ItemMaster --> DB 425 Tracking --> DB 426 Sync["Data Sync"] --> DB 427 Sync <--> CL["Coldlion"] 428 ``` 429 430 ## End-to-End Flows 431 432 ### Sign-In and Identity 433 434 Password login and Microsoft SSO both end by storing a Designflow RS256 JWT in 435 the browser. For SSO, the browser obtains an Azure ID token, posts it to 436 `/api/core/auth/azure`, and the core backend verifies Azure JWKS before signing a 437 Designflow JWT. 438 439 Every subsequent browser request sends the Designflow JWT to the BFF. 440 441 ### RFQ and Vendor Quoting 442 443 RFQ is primarily a frontend + core-backend workflow: 444 445 1. Frontend loads grid layout and RFQ rows from core backend. 446 2. Frontend applies AG Grid column definitions and calculation value setters. 447 3. User edits RFQ item fields or vendor quote fields. 448 4. Frontend recalculates landed cost, margins, duty, container cost, and sales 449 pricing where relevant. 450 5. Core backend persists RFQ item/vendor changes. 451 6. Brevo may send quote/signup/request emails. 452 7. Vendor users access filtered RFQ data through the same BFF/core path. 453 454 RFQ also reaches into other domains: 455 456 - Factory country data is used by tariff calculations. 457 - Item images/attachments may be stored in DigitalOcean Spaces. 458 - Grid layouts and view states are shared system metadata. 459 - Standardized products can export/import to RFQ. 460 461 ### Item Master and Art Piece 462 463 Item Library, Item Detail, and Art Piece screens are frontend modules that route 464 through `/api/item_master`. 465 466 The item-master service owns the active API boundary for these screens. It reads 467 and writes item master tables, while data-sync can populate or refresh item data 468 from Coldlion into the same schema family. 469 470 ### Production and Licensing Tracking 471 472 Production and licensing tracking route through `/api/tracking`. 473 474 Production tracking works with production order tables and factory-facing order 475 date updates. Licensing tracking works with licensing status/milestone/grouping 476 data and item license images. The tracking service builds startup caches for 477 licensing/factory lead-time calculations. 478 479 ### Sample Tracking 480 481 Sample Tracking is a newer tracking module under `/api/tracking/sample`. It has 482 its own tables and state machine rather than using `itemHeader` directly as the 483 primary record. It links to the broader PLM through: 484 485 - `item_id_fk` 486 - `prod_order_no_fk` 487 - factory/group references 488 - sample events 489 - sample boxes 490 - sample attachments/photos 491 492 The server is authoritative for sample status transitions. 493 494 ### Lead-Time Calculation 495 496 The frontend calls `/api/tracking/lead-time/*`. The tracking service uses design 497 team time, licensing time, factory time, merch group descriptions, and fuzzy 498 matching helpers to calculate lead time and needed dates. 499 500 ### HTS, Tariffs, and AI Classification 501 502 The frontend duty-rate tools call core backend admin/openrouter/cross routes. 503 504 Core backend: 505 506 - proxies OpenRouter chat/model calls 507 - proxies CBP CROSS search 508 - looks up bundled MFN HTS rates 509 - manages Section 301 upload/lookup state 510 - manages Trump 2025 country-rate settings 511 - stores AI/tariff app settings 512 513 The model suggests classification. The system derives actual rates from 514 authoritative lookup sources and admin-maintained settings. 515 516 ### Coldlion Sync 517 518 Data Sync is the Coldlion integration layer. It is API-key protected and intended 519 for service/system usage. 520 521 It pulls external data into the same database schema used by the application 522 services. That makes sync a background/source-of-truth feeder for item, 523 merch-group, vendor/customer, production-order, and prepack-association data. 524 525 ## Operational Notes 526 527 - The app has no single OpenAPI contract across all services. 528 - Many routes are internal Angular-shaped APIs and return domain rows or 529 grid-specific payloads. 530 - BFF routes are stable as a gateway, but service contracts remain mixed 531 legacy/new style. 532 - The shared database is the strongest cross-service coupling. 533 - Additive schema changes are commonly handled by startup Sequelize sync and 534 idempotent inline migrations rather than a dedicated migration repository. 535 - JWT is stored in browser `localStorage`, not an HTTP-only cookie. 536 - DigitalOcean direct browser upload uses a presigned PUT followed by an 537 explicit `setObjectPublic` call. 538 539 ## Canonical Local Ports 540 541 | Service | Repo | Local port | 542 |---|---|---:| 543 | Frontend | `designflow-frontend` | 4200 | 544 | BFF | `designflow-bff` | 5004 | 545 | Core backend | `designflow-backend` | 5000 | 546 | Data sync | `designflow-data-syncing` | 5001 | 547 | Tracking | `designflow-tracking` | 5002 | 548 | Item master | `designflow-item-master` | 5003 | 549 550 ## Canonical BFF Service Prefixes 551 552 | Browser path prefix | Target service | 553 |---|---| 554 | `/api/core/*` | `designflow-backend` | 555 | `/api/item_master/*` | `designflow-item-master` | 556 | `/api/tracking/*` | `designflow-tracking` | 557 | `/api/data_sync/*` | `designflow-data-syncing` | 558 559 ## Where To Change Things 560 561 Use this as a first-pass guide: 562 563 - Frontend-only layout, grid behavior, labels, navigation: `designflow-frontend` 564 - RFQ data, vendor quoting, users, roles, admin settings, tariffs, SSO: 565 `designflow-backend` 566 - Item Library / Item Detail / Art Piece APIs: `designflow-item-master` 567 - Production / licensing / sample / lead-time APIs: `designflow-tracking` 568 - Coldlion import/sync behavior: `designflow-data-syncing` 569 - API routing, deployed service target, Cloud Run OIDC forwarding, CORS: 570 `designflow-bff` 571 572

All files read (source, tests, configs, and all six .md docs). Findings below.

# Code Review: designflow-bff

## High severity

*(none ŌĆö no outright broken logic or exploitable hole found; the riskiest items are the two below, which are borderline high)*

## Medium severity

1. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\src\server.js:9-11`** ŌĆö When `CORS_ORIGINS` is unset, CORS falls back to `origin: true` with `credentials: true`, which reflects *any* origin and allows credentialed cross-site requests; a missing env var in a deployed environment silently opens the gateway to all websites instead of failing. Confidence: certain (behavior), likely (real-world impact, since auth is bearer-token not cookie based).

2. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\src\backendProxy.js:30-31`** ŌĆö A new ID-token client is created and `fetchIdToken` is called on **every proxied request** with no caching; google-auth-library does not cache tokens on this code path, so each API call adds a metadata-server round trip (and a potential per-request failure point) for a token that is valid for ~1 hour. Confidence: likely.

3. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\routes\api.js:10-11`** ŌĆö `proxyTimeout: 0, timeout: 0` disables timeouts entirely; a hung backend keeps the client connection (and Cloud Run instance concurrency slot) open indefinitely until the client or Cloud Run's own platform timeout gives up. Confidence: likely.

4. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\.gitignore` + `Dockerfile:12`** ŌĆö Both `package-lock.json` and `yarn.lock` are gitignored, and the Docker build runs `yarn install` with no lockfile, so every production build resolves dependency versions fresh; a bad upstream release can break or compromise a deploy with no code change. Confidence: certain.

5. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\package.json:7-9` + `Dockerfile:16`** ŌĆö Cloud Run containers start via `yarn start:$NODE_ENV`, which re-runs the full Jest suite and then launches **nodemon** (a dev file-watcher) in sandbox environments on every cold start; production (`start:production`) also re-runs tests at container boot. Slower cold starts and a dev tool supervising production-like traffic. Confidence: certain.

6. **Design note ŌĆö `routes/api.js` (whole file)** ŌĆö The BFF attaches its own OIDC token to any anonymous request, so all four "private" (`--no-allow-unauthenticated`) backends are effectively public through `/api/:service/*`; security rests entirely on each backend route having its own JWT/API-key middleware ŌĆö any backend route that forgets `authRole` is internet-exposed. This matches the documented architecture, but the BFF performs zero validation of its own. Confidence: certain (mechanism).

## Low severity

7. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\src\server.js:29-34`** ŌĆö Comment says "Fail fast if critical env vars are missing" but the code only `console.warn`s and starts anyway; a misconfigured service boots and returns confusing 404s/proxy errors instead of refusing to start (the exact failure mode behind the repo's repeated login incidents). Confidence: certain.

8. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\routes\api.js:57-58`** ŌĆö Catch block uses axios-style `err.response?.status` / `err.response?.data` and sends `err.message` to the browser; google-auth errors aren't shaped this way for data, so clients receive raw internal error text (minor information leak, inconsistent error contract vs. the proxy's `{error: "Bad gateway"}`). Confidence: likely.

9. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\src\serviceMap.js:2-7`** ŌĆö `SERVICE_MAP` reads `process.env` at require time; it only works because `server.js` happens to require `config/env.js` one line earlier, so any other entry point (tests, scripts) requiring it first gets all-undefined URLs. Fragile implicit ordering. Confidence: certain.

10. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\e2e\example.spec.js`** ŌĆö Dead code: the only Playwright "e2e tests" are the stock boilerplate that tests playwright.dev, not this app, yet three npm scripts and a 3-browser config exist for it. Confidence: certain.

11. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\jest.config.js:4` + `tests/unit/sanity.test.js`** ŌĆö The Jest suite that gates every CI build contains exactly one test: `expect(1+1).toBe(2)`; the actual proxy/auth logic has zero automated coverage, so "tests must pass before deploy" provides no real protection. Confidence: certain.

12. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\package.json:32`** ŌĆö `jest` is in `dependencies` instead of `devDependencies`, bloating the production image. Confidence: certain.

13. **`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-bff\routes\api.js:34-36`** ŌĆö The 404 response echoes the user-supplied service segment back in the error body; harmless as JSON but unnecessary input reflection. Confidence: certain (mechanism), possible (impact).

## Documentation problems

14. **`README.md:128`** (low, certain) ŌĆö Documents `GET /health ŌåÆ { status: 'ok' }` but `routes/health.js:5-9` actually returns `{ status: "UP", service, env }`.

15. **`README.md:96-97` vs `package.json:7`** (low, certain) ŌĆö Local-dev instructions say create `.env.local` and run with `NODE_ENV=local`, but `npm start` uses `NODE_ENV=localhost` and therefore loads `.env.localhost`; following the README then running `npm start` loads nothing.

16. **`README.md:112`** (low, certain) ŌĆö "Each script runs `npm test` first, then starts `nodemon`" ŌĆö false for `start:production`, which uses plain `node`.

17. **`.gitignore:2,5,6` vs git** (low, certain) ŌĆö `.env.production` and `.env.sandbox` are listed in `.gitignore` yet are tracked and committed in `env/`; the ignore intent and reality contradict each other (no secrets inside today ŌĆö only URLs ŌĆö but the confusion invites accidentally committing one that does have secrets).

18. **`AGENTS.md:17` and `docs/system-architecture.md:4`** (low, certain) ŌĆö Both reference the workspace at `D:\repos\dflow` (and a memory file under `D--repos-dflow`), but the repo now lives at `C:\repos\dflow plm`; the incident-playbook pointer is a dead path.

19. **`AGENTS.md:49` / `README.md:57`** (low, certain) ŌĆö Both list `env/.env.sandbox-albert` etc. plus `.env.example`; accurate, but AGENTS.md ┬¦2 omits that `.env.localhost` is gitignored while the others are committed ŌĆö minor, paired with finding 17.

## Overall assessment

This is a small, focused proxy whose core logic (service map ŌåÆ OIDC token ŌåÆ header swap ŌåÆ http-proxy) is sound and matches its unusually good architecture/incident documentation ŌĆö the docs are this repo's strongest asset. The real weaknesses are operational rather than logical: per-request token fetching, no timeouts, no lockfile, nodemon + test-run at container boot, and a test suite that gates CI while testing nothing. No exploitable vulnerability was found, but the permissive CORS fallback and warn-only env validation are exactly the kind of silent-misconfiguration traps that already caused this repo's three recorded login outages.
